examples.title: Calculate nCr using Pascal's Triangle
examples.description: Program to compute combinations using dynamic programming based on Pascal's Triangle identity: C(n,r) = C(n-1,r-1) + C(n-1,r)
examples.code:
FUNCTION nCr(n, r):
 IF r == 0 OR r == n THEN
 RETURN 1
 ELSE IF r > n THEN
 RETURN 0
 ELSE
 RETURN nCr(n-1, r-1) + nCr(n-1, r)
 END IF
END FUNCTION

// Alternative: Iterative calculation
FUNCTION nCr_iterative(n, r):
 IF r > n-r THEN
 r = n-r // Use symmetry property
 END IF
 result = 1
 FOR i = 1 TO r DO
 result = result * (n - r + i) / i
 END FOR
 RETURN result
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Uses the recursive property of Pascal's Triangle: each combination can be computed from smaller combinations. The iterative version avoids overflow and is more efficient with symmetry optimization.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n×r) for DP, O(r) for iterative

examples.title: Generate All Permutations
examples.description: Algorithm to generate all permutations of n elements using backtracking
examples.code:
FUNCTION Permute(arr, left, right):
 IF left == right THEN
 PRINT arr // One permutation complete
 ELSE
 FOR i = left TO right DO
 Swap(arr[left], arr[i])
 Permute(arr, left + 1, right)
 Swap(arr[left], arr[i]) // Backtrack
 END FOR
 END IF
END FUNCTION

// Example usage for 3 elements: [1,2,3]
// Permute([1,2,3], 0, 2) generates all 3! = 6 permutations
examples.explanation: Uses recursion and backtracking to generate all permutations. Each element gets placed in each position systematically, exploring all possible orderings.

examples.title: Stars and Bars Distribution
examples.description: Calculate number of ways to distribute n identical items into r distinct boxes using stars and bars
examples.explanation: Stars and Bars formula counts ways to place separators (bars) among identical items (stars). With n stars and r boxes, we need r-1 bars to create r partitions.

examples.title: Pigeonhole Principle Check
examples.description: Function to determine minimum guaranteed repetitions using pigeonhole principle
examples.explanation: Pigeonhole principle states that when n items are placed into m containers and n > m, at least one container has ⌈n/m⌉ items. For guaranteeing repetition, we need containers + 1 items.
